expanded_description: A comprehensive guide to Lewis dot diagrams, the visual representation system for valence electrons that enables prediction of bonding patterns, ion formation, and molecular structure for all main-group elements.

core_concept.principle: Lewis dot diagrams represent valence electrons as dots around element symbols, providing a visual method to predict how atoms will bond to achieve stable electron configurations.
core_concept.explanation: Lewis dot diagrams (also called Lewis symbols or electron dot structures) show only the valence electrons of an atom—the electrons in the outermost shell that participate in chemical bonding. By representing these electrons as dots around the element symbol, we can quickly determine how many bonds an atom can form, whether it will gain or lose electrons, and what type of compounds it will create. This simple visual tool, developed by Gilbert N. Lewis in 1916, remains one of the most powerful predictive tools in chemistry for understanding bonding behavior.

image.alt: Periodic table showing Lewis dot symbols for main group elements
image.caption: Lewis dot symbols for Period 2 and 3 elements: dots represent valence electrons available for bonding.

sections.introduction: Before drawing Lewis dot diagrams, you must understand valence electrons—the electrons that determine an atom's chemical behavior and bonding capacity. These are the only electrons shown in Lewis structures.

key_concept.definition: Valence electrons are the electrons in the outermost principal energy level (valence shell) of an atom; these electrons participate in chemical bonding and determine an element's chemical properties.
key_concept.context: For main-group elements (Groups 1-2, 13-18), the number of valence electrons equals the group number (Groups 1-2) or group number minus 10 (Groups 13-18). Transition metals have more complex valence electron patterns.

principles.statement: For main-group elements, the group number directly indicates the number of valence electrons: Group 1 = 1 valence e⁻, Group 2 = 2 valence e⁻, Group 13 = 3 valence e⁻, etc.
principles.application: Enables quick determination of valence electrons without writing full electron configurations; essential for drawing Lewis dot diagrams.
principles.example: Carbon (Group 14) has 4 valence electrons; Oxygen (Group 16) has 6 valence electrons; Chlorine (Group 17) has 7 valence electrons.
principles.analogy: Like a person's available hands: valence electrons are the 'hands' an atom uses to 'shake hands' (bond) with other atoms.

image.alt: Chart showing group number to valence electron relationship
image.caption: Group number determines valence electrons for main-group elements.

principles.statement: Core electrons (inner shell electrons) do not participate in bonding and are not shown in Lewis dot diagrams; only valence electrons are represented.
principles.application: Simplifies bonding predictions by focusing only on reactive electrons; explains why elements in the same group have similar chemistry.
principles.example: Sodium: [Ne]3s¹ has 10 core electrons ([Ne]) and 1 valence electron (3s¹); only the 1 valence electron appears in Lewis dot diagram.
principles.analogy: Like a tree: core electrons are the roots and trunk (stable, internal); valence electrons are the branches and leaves (interact with environment).

image.alt: Diagram comparing core and valence electrons in an atom
image.caption: Core electrons (inner shells) vs. valence electrons (outer shell): only valence electrons participate in bonding.

principles.statement: Atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve 8 valence electrons (octet rule), the stable configuration of noble gases.
principles.application: Predicts ion formation (metals lose, nonmetals gain) and covalent bond counts; explains why noble gases are unreactive.
principles.example: Neon (Group 18) has 8 valence electrons (2s²2p⁶) and is inert; Chlorine (Group 17) has 7 valence electrons and gains 1 to achieve neon-like stability.
principles.analogy: Like a complete puzzle: atoms 'collect' electrons until their valence shell puzzle has all 8 pieces (except H and He which need 2).

image.alt: Noble gas electron configurations showing complete valence shells
image.caption: Noble gases have complete valence shells (8 electrons), making them chemically stable and unreactive.

components.definition: Valence electron count determines bonding behavior and Lewis dot diagram appearance
components.interpretation: Group 1: 1 valence e⁻; Group 2: 2 valence e⁻; Group 13: 3 valence e⁻; Group 14: 4 valence e⁻; Group 15: 5 valence e⁻; Group 16: 6 valence e⁻; Group 17: 7 valence e⁻; Group 18: 8 valence e⁻ (except He: 2)

worked_examples.title: Determining Valence Electrons from Periodic Position
worked_examples.problem: Determine the number of valence electrons for: (a) magnesium, (b) phosphorus, (c) bromine. Write the electron configuration showing valence electrons.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the group number for each element.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Mg: Group 2; (b) P: Group 15; (c) Br: Group 17

step_by_step.explanation: Apply valence electron formula based on group.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Group 2 → 2 valence e⁻; (b) Group 15 → 15-10 = 5 valence e⁻; (c) Group 17 → 17-10 = 7 valence e⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Write electron configurations highlighting valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Mg: [Ne]3s² (valence: 3s²); (b) P: [Ne]3s²3p³ (valence: 3s²3p³); (c) Br: [Ar]4s²3d¹⁰4p⁵ (valence: 4s²4p⁵)

step_by_step.explanation: Verify valence electron count from configuration.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Magnesium: 2 valence electrons (3s²). (b) Phosphorus: 5 valence electrons (3s²3p³). (c) Bromine: 7 valence electrons (4s²4p⁵). Group number directly indicates valence electron count for main-group elements, enabling quick Lewis dot diagram construction.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using group number to determine valence electron count for Lewis dot diagrams

sections.section_title: Drawing Lewis Dot Symbols for Elements
sections.introduction: Lewis dot symbols provide a simple visual representation of an element's valence electrons. Learning the systematic method for drawing these symbols enables prediction of bonding patterns and chemical behavior.

key_concept.definition: A Lewis dot symbol consists of the element's chemical symbol surrounded by dots representing valence electrons, with dots placed singly on four sides (top, right, bottom, left) before pairing.
key_concept.context: The arrangement of dots follows a specific convention that reflects orbital filling: first four electrons are placed singly on each side; additional electrons pair up with existing dots.

classifications.value: 1 valence electron shown as single dot
characteristics: Readily loses electron to form +1 ion
classifications.behavior: Single dot represents the single s-electron that is easily lost; explains why these elements form +1 cations.

examples.explanation: Na with one dot: $\cdot\text{Na}$. The single dot represents the 3s¹ valence electron. Na loses this electron to form Na⁺ with [Ne] configuration.

image.alt: Lewis dot symbol for sodium showing one valence electron
image.caption: Sodium Lewis symbol: one dot represents single valence electron easily lost to form Na⁺.

examples.explanation: K with one dot: $\cdot\text{K}$. Same pattern as Na but with 4s¹ valence electron. Forms K⁺ ion.
Li with one dot: $\cdot\text{Li}$. Smallest alkali metal with 2s¹ valence electron. Forms Li⁺ ion.

classifications.value: 2 valence electrons shown as two single dots or one pair
characteristics: Loses both electrons to form +2 ion
classifications.behavior: Two dots represent the two s-electrons that are lost together; explains why these elements form +2 cations.

examples.explanation: Mg with two dots: $\cdot\text{Mg}\cdot$ or $:\text{Mg}$. The two dots represent 3s² valence electrons. Mg loses both to form Mg²⁺ with [Ne] configuration.

image.alt: Lewis dot symbol for magnesium showing two valence electrons
image.caption: Magnesium Lewis symbol: two dots represent two valence electrons lost to form Mg²⁺.

examples.explanation: Ca with two dots: $\cdot\text{Ca}\cdot$. Same pattern as Mg but with 4s² valence electrons. Forms Ca²⁺ ion.

classifications.value: 3-4 valence electrons with increasing unpaired dots
classifications.behavior: Unpaired dots represent bonding sites; Group 14 (4 dots) can form 4 covalent bonds to complete octet.

examples.explanation: B with three dots: $\cdot\dot{\text{B}}\cdot$. Three unpaired dots represent 2s²2p¹ valence electrons. Often forms 3 bonds with incomplete octet (6 e⁻).

image.alt: Lewis dot symbol for boron showing three valence electrons
image.caption: Boron Lewis symbol: three dots, often forms compounds with incomplete octet.

examples.explanation: C with four dots: $\cdot\dot{\text{C}}\cdot$. Four unpaired dots represent 2s²2p² valence electrons. Forms 4 covalent bonds to complete octet (8 e⁻).

image.alt: Lewis dot symbol for carbon showing four valence electrons
image.caption: Carbon Lewis symbol: four unpaired dots, forms 4 covalent bonds in organic compounds.

classifications.value: 5-7 valence electrons with some paired and some unpaired
characteristics: Gain electrons to form anions or share in covalent bonds
classifications.behavior: Unpaired dots show bonding capacity; paired dots are lone pairs that don't bond but affect molecular shape.

examples.explanation: N with five dots: $:\dot{\text{N}}\cdot$. Three unpaired dots (bonding sites) + one lone pair. Forms 3 covalent bonds to complete octet.

image.alt: Lewis dot symbol for nitrogen showing five valence electrons
image.caption: Nitrogen Lewis symbol: 3 unpaired dots for bonding, 1 lone pair.

examples.explanation: O with six dots: $:\ddot{\text{O}}\cdot$. Two unpaired dots (bonding sites) + two lone pairs. Forms 2 covalent bonds to complete octet.

image.alt: Lewis dot symbol for oxygen showing six valence electrons
image.caption: Oxygen Lewis symbol: 2 unpaired dots for bonding, 2 lone pairs.

examples.explanation: Cl with seven dots: $:\ddot{\text{Cl}}\cdot$. One unpaired dot (bonding site) + three lone pairs. Gains 1 electron to form Cl⁻ or forms 1 covalent bond.

image.alt: Lewis dot symbol for chlorine showing seven valence electrons
image.caption: Chlorine Lewis symbol: 1 unpaired dot for bonding, 3 lone pairs.

classifications.value: 8 valence electrons (complete octet) shown as four pairs
classifications.behavior: All electrons paired; no unpaired dots means no bonding sites; explains noble gas inertness.

examples.explanation: Ne with eight dots: $:\ddot{\text{Ne}}:$. Four pairs represent complete 2s²2p⁶ valence shell. No unpaired electrons = no bonding = inert.

image.alt: Lewis dot symbol for neon showing eight valence electrons
image.caption: Neon Lewis symbol: complete octet (4 pairs), no unpaired electrons, chemically inert.

examples.explanation: He with two dots: $:\text{He}$. Only needs 2 electrons (duet rule) to fill 1s orbital. Complete valence shell = inert.

image.alt: Lewis dot symbol for helium showing two valence electrons
image.caption: Helium Lewis symbol: duet (2 e⁻) fills 1s orbital, chemically inert.

principles.statement: Place the first four valence electrons as single dots on the four sides (top, right, bottom, left) of the element symbol; pair up additional electrons with existing dots.
principles.application: Ensures consistent Lewis dot diagrams; unpaired dots represent bonding sites; paired dots represent lone pairs.
principles.example: Carbon (4 e⁻): $\cdot\dot{\text{C}}\cdot$ (all unpaired); Nitrogen (5 e⁻): $:\dot{\text{N}}\cdot$ (3 unpaired, 1 pair); Oxygen (6 e⁻): $:\ddot{\text{O}}\cdot$ (2 unpaired, 2 pairs).
principles.analogy: Like seating at a square table: first 4 people sit one per side; additional people must share seats (pair up).

image.alt: Diagram showing dot placement convention for Lewis symbols
image.caption: Dot placement: first 4 electrons placed singly on four sides; additional electrons pair up.

principles.statement: The number of unpaired dots in a Lewis symbol indicates how many covalent bonds an atom typically forms to complete its octet.
principles.application: Predicts molecular formulas: C (4 unpaired) forms 4 bonds (CH₄); N (3 unpaired) forms 3 bonds (NH₃); O (2 unpaired) forms 2 bonds (H₂O).
principles.example: Carbon: 4 unpaired dots → 4 bonds in CH₄; Nitrogen: 3 unpaired dots → 3 bonds in NH₃; Oxygen: 2 unpaired dots → 2 bonds in H₂O.
principles.analogy: Like handshake offers: unpaired dots are 'hands available' for bonding; paired dots are 'hands already holding' lone pairs.

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Dot Symbols for Period 3 Elements
worked_examples.problem: Draw Lewis dot symbols for all Period 3 elements: Na, Mg, Al, Si, P, S, Cl, Ar. Identify the number of unpaired electrons for each.

step_by_step.explanation: Determine valence electrons from group number for each element.
step_by_step.calculation: Na (Group 1): 1 e⁻; Mg (Group 2): 2 e⁻; Al (Group 13): 3 e⁻; Si (Group 14): 4 e⁻; P (Group 15): 5 e⁻; S (Group 16): 6 e⁻; Cl (Group 17): 7 e⁻; Ar (Group 18): 8 e⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Draw Lewis symbols following dot placement convention (single dots first, then pair).
step_by_step.calculation: Na: $\cdot\text{Na}$; Mg: $\cdot\text{Mg}\cdot$; Al: $\cdot\dot{\text{Al}}\cdot$; Si: $\cdot\dot{\text{Si}}\cdot$; P: $:\dot{\text{P}}\cdot$; S: $:\ddot{\text{S}}\cdot$; Cl: $:\ddot{\text{Cl}}\cdot$; Ar: $:\ddot{\text{Ar}}:$

step_by_step.explanation: Count unpaired electrons for each symbol.
step_by_step.calculation: Na: 1 unpaired; Mg: 2 unpaired; Al: 3 unpaired; Si: 4 unpaired; P: 3 unpaired; S: 2 unpaired; Cl: 1 unpaired; Ar: 0 unpaired

step_by_step.explanation: Relate unpaired electrons to bonding capacity.
step_by_step.calculation: Unpaired = bonding sites: Na loses 1 e⁻; Mg loses 2 e⁻; Al loses 3 e⁻; Si shares 4 e⁻; P shares 3 e⁻; S shares 2 e⁻; Cl gains/shares 1 e⁻; Ar doesn't bond

worked_examples.final_answer: Period 3 Lewis symbols: Na (1 dot, 1 unpaired), Mg (2 dots, 2 unpaired), Al (3 dots, 3 unpaired), Si (4 dots, 4 unpaired), P (5 dots, 3 unpaired + 1 pair), S (6 dots, 2 unpaired + 2 pairs), Cl (7 dots, 1 unpaired + 3 pairs), Ar (8 dots, 0 unpaired + 4 pairs). Unpaired electrons indicate bonding capacity; paired electrons are lone pairs.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic Lewis dot symbol construction for entire period showing bonding pattern trends

sections.section_title: Predicting Ion Formation from Lewis Symbols
sections.introduction: Lewis dot diagrams not only show valence electrons but also predict how atoms will form ions. By examining the number of valence electrons, we can determine whether an atom will lose or gain electrons to achieve a stable noble gas configuration.

key_concept.definition: Lewis symbols predict ion formation: metals (1-3 valence e⁻) lose electrons to form cations; nonmetals (5-7 valence e⁻) gain electrons to form anions; both achieve noble gas configurations.
key_concept.context: The octet rule drives ion formation: atoms gain or lose electrons to achieve 8 valence electrons (or 2 for H and He), matching the stable configuration of noble gases.

classifications.value: Metals lose valence electrons shown as dots, forming positive ions
classifications.behavior: All valence dots are removed; ion has no dots (empty valence shell exposes previous noble gas core).

examples.explanation: Na: $\cdot\text{Na}$ (1 valence e⁻) → Na⁺: $[\text{Na}]^+$ (0 valence e⁻ shown). Loses 1 electron to achieve [Ne] configuration. Charge +1 indicated outside brackets.

image.alt: Lewis symbol transformation from Na atom to Na⁺ ion
image.caption: Na to Na⁺: valence electron removed, charge indicated, noble gas configuration achieved.

examples.explanation: Mg: $\cdot\text{Mg}\cdot$ (2 valence e⁻) → Mg²⁺: $[\text{Mg}]^{2+}$ (0 valence e⁻ shown). Loses 2 electrons to achieve [Ne] configuration. Charge +2 indicated.

image.alt: Lewis symbol transformation from Mg atom to Mg²⁺ ion
image.caption: Mg to Mg²⁺: both valence electrons removed, +2 charge indicated.

examples.explanation: Al: $\cdot\dot{\text{Al}}\cdot$ (3 valence e⁻) → Al³⁺: $[\text{Al}]^{3+}$ (0 valence e⁻ shown). Loses 3 electrons to achieve [Ne] configuration. Charge +3 indicated.

image.alt: Lewis symbol transformation from Al atom to Al³⁺ ion
image.caption: Al to Al³⁺: all 3 valence electrons removed, +3 charge indicated.

classifications.value: Nonmetals gain electrons to complete octet, forming negative ions
classifications.behavior: Dots are added to complete octet (8 total); charge indicated outside brackets; all electrons shown including gained ones.

examples.explanation: Cl: $:\ddot{\text{Cl}}\cdot$ (7 valence e⁻) → Cl⁻: $[:\ddot{\text{Cl}}:]^-$ (8 valence e⁻). Gains 1 electron to achieve [Ar] configuration. Complete octet shown with 8 dots.

image.alt: Lewis symbol transformation from Cl atom to Cl⁻ ion
image.caption: Cl to Cl⁻: one electron added to complete octet, -1 charge indicated.

examples.explanation: O: $:\ddot{\text{O}}\cdot$ (6 valence e⁻) → O²⁻: $[:\ddot{\text{O}}:]^{2-}$ (8 valence e⁻). Gains 2 electrons to achieve [Ne] configuration. Complete octet with 8 dots.

image.alt: Lewis symbol transformation from O atom to O²⁻ ion
image.caption: O to O²⁻: two electrons added to complete octet, -2 charge indicated.

examples.explanation: N: $:\dot{\text{N}}\cdot$ (5 valence e⁻) → N³⁻: $[:\dot{\text{N}}:]^{3-}$ (8 valence e⁻). Gains 3 electrons to achieve [Ne] configuration. Complete octet with 8 dots.

image.alt: Lewis symbol transformation from N atom to N³⁻ ion
image.caption: N to N³⁻: three electrons added to complete octet, -3 charge indicated.

principles.statement: Ions are shown in square brackets with the charge indicated as a superscript outside the brackets; cations show no dots (lost valence electrons), anions show complete octet (8 dots).
principles.application: Standard notation distinguishes ions from neutral atoms; brackets indicate the charge applies to the entire species.
principles.example: Na⁺: $[\text{Na}]^+$ (no dots); Cl⁻: $[:\ddot{\text{Cl}}:]^-$ (8 dots); Mg²⁺: $[\text{Mg}]^{2+}$ (no dots); O²⁻: $[:\ddot{\text{O}}:]^{2-}$ (8 dots).
principles.analogy: Like a name tag: brackets are the 'tag' showing this is an ion; superscript charge shows how many electrons were lost or gained.

principles.statement: Ions with the same number of electrons (isoelectronic) have identical Lewis dot diagrams but different nuclear charges and chemical properties.
principles.application: Explains why Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺, O²⁻, F⁻, and N³⁻ all have the same Lewis structure (8 dots like Ne) but different charges and sizes.
principles.example: Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺, O²⁻, F⁻, N³⁻ all have [Ne] configuration (8 valence e⁻ shown for anions, 0 for cations) but different charges (+1, +2, +3, -2, -1, -3).

image.alt: Lewis symbols for isoelectronic ions showing same electron configuration
image.caption: Isoelectronic series: different elements, same electron configuration (10 electrons like Ne).

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Symbols for Ions
worked_examples.problem: Draw Lewis dot symbols for the ions formed by: (a) potassium, (b) calcium, (c) sulfur, (d) fluorine. Show the charge on each ion.

step_by_step.explanation: Determine the neutral atom's valence electrons from group number.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) K: Group 1 → 1 valence e⁻; (b) Ca: Group 2 → 2 valence e⁻; (c) S: Group 16 → 6 valence e⁻; (d) F: Group 17 → 7 valence e⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Determine whether atom loses or gains electrons to achieve octet.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) K: lose 1 e⁻ → K⁺; (b) Ca: lose 2 e⁻ → Ca²⁺; (c) S: gain 2 e⁻ → S²⁻; (d) F: gain 1 e⁻ → F⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Draw Lewis symbols for ions with appropriate dots and charges.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) K⁺: $[\text{K}]^+$ (no dots); (b) Ca²⁺: $[\text{Ca}]^{2+}$ (no dots); (c) S²⁻: $[:\ddot{\text{S}}:]^{2-}$ (8 dots); (d) F⁻: $[:\ddot{\text{F}}:]^-$ (8 dots)

step_by_step.explanation: Verify noble gas configuration for each ion.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) K⁺: $[\text{K}]^+$ (lost 1 e⁻, [Ar] config). (b) Ca²⁺: $[\text{Ca}]^{2+}$ (lost 2 e⁻, [Ar] config). (c) S²⁻: $[:\ddot{\text{S}}:]^{2-}$ (gained 2 e⁻, 8 dots, [Ar] config). (d) F⁻: $[:\ddot{\text{F}}:]^-$ (gained 1 e⁻, 8 dots, [Ne] config). Cations show no dots; anions show complete octet.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using Lewis symbols to represent ion formation with proper notation and charge indication

sections.section_title: Using Lewis Symbols to Predict Bonding
sections.introduction: Lewis dot diagrams are most powerful when used to predict how atoms will bond together. By examining unpaired electrons in Lewis symbols, we can determine the number and type of bonds atoms will form.

key_concept.definition: Unpaired dots in Lewis symbols represent bonding sites; atoms bond by sharing or transferring unpaired electrons to achieve complete octets for all atoms involved.
key_concept.context: This predictive power enables determination of molecular formulas (H₂O, not HO), bond types (single, double, triple), and molecular structures before drawing full Lewis structures.

principles.statement: The number of unpaired dots indicates how many covalent bonds an atom will form; atoms share unpaired electrons to complete octets.
principles.application: Predicts molecular formulas: C (4 unpaired) + 4H (1 unpaired each) → CH₄; N (3 unpaired) + 3H → NH₃; O (2 unpaired) + 2H → H₂O.
principles.example: Carbon (4 unpaired dots) forms 4 bonds in CH₄; Nitrogen (3 unpaired dots) forms 3 bonds in NH₃; Oxygen (2 unpaired dots) forms 2 bonds in H₂O.
principles.analogy: Like puzzle pieces: unpaired dots are 'connection points'; atoms connect until all connection points are used and all pieces are complete (octets).

image.alt: Lewis symbols showing how unpaired electrons predict covalent bonding
image.caption: Covalent bonding prediction: unpaired dots show bonding capacity; atoms share to complete octets.

principles.statement: Metals (1-3 unpaired dots) transfer electrons to nonmetals (5-7 valence e⁻, need 1-3 to complete octet); opposite charges attract.
principles.application: Predicts ionic compound formulas: Na (1 e⁻ to lose) + Cl (1 e⁻ to gain) → NaCl; Mg (2 e⁻ to lose) + 2Cl → MgCl₂.
principles.example: Na (1 dot) transfers to Cl (7 dots, needs 1): NaCl. Mg (2 dots) transfers to 2 Cl atoms: MgCl₂. Al (3 dots) + 3 Cl: AlCl₃.

image.alt: Lewis symbols showing electron transfer in ionic bonding
image.caption: Ionic bonding prediction: metals transfer electrons to nonmetals; charges balance in compound.

principles.statement: When single bonds don't complete octets, atoms form double or triple bonds by sharing additional electron pairs.
principles.application: Explains O=O in O₂ (each O needs 2 e⁻), C=O in CO₂ (C needs 4 e⁻, each O needs 2 e⁻), N≡N in N₂ (each N needs 3 e⁻).
principles.example: O₂: each O has 2 unpaired dots → share 2 pairs → O=O double bond. CO₂: C has 4 unpaired, each O has 2 → O=C=O with two double bonds. N₂: each N has 3 unpaired → share 3 pairs → N≡N triple bond.

image.alt: Lewis symbols showing prediction of double and triple bonds
image.caption: Multiple bond prediction: when single bonds don't complete octets, atoms share additional pairs.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Molecular Formulas from Lewis Symbols
worked_examples.problem: Use Lewis dot symbols to predict the molecular formulas for compounds formed by: (a) carbon and hydrogen, (b) nitrogen and hydrogen, (c) phosphorus and chlorine.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw Lewis symbols for each element and count unpaired electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) C: 4 unpaired dots, H: 1 unpaired dot; (b) N: 3 unpaired dots, H: 1 unpaired dot; (c) P: 3 unpaired dots, Cl: 1 unpaired dot

step_by_step.explanation: Determine how many atoms needed to use all unpaired electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) C needs 4 bonds → 4 H atoms; (b) N needs 3 bonds → 3 H atoms; (c) P needs 3 bonds → 3 Cl atoms

step_by_step.explanation: Write molecular formulas based on bonding requirements.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) CH₄ (methane); (b) NH₃ (ammonia); (c) PCl₃ (phosphorus trichloride)

step_by_step.explanation: Verify octet completion for all atoms in predicted molecules.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) C: 8 e⁻ (4 bonds), H: 2 e⁻ each ✓; (b) N: 8 e⁻ (3 bonds + 1 lone pair), H: 2 e⁻ each ✓; (c) P: 8 e⁻ (3 bonds + 1 lone pair), Cl: 8 e⁻ each ✓

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) CH₄: Carbon (4 unpaired) bonds with 4 hydrogen atoms (1 unpaired each). (b) NH₃: Nitrogen (3 unpaired) bonds with 3 hydrogen atoms. (c) PCl₃: Phosphorus (3 unpaired) bonds with 3 chlorine atoms (1 unpaired each). Lewis symbols predict molecular formulas by matching unpaired electrons for bonding.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using Lewis dot symbols to predict molecular formulas from unpaired electron counts

worked_examples.title: Predicting Ionic Compound Formulas from Lewis Symbols
worked_examples.problem: Use Lewis dot symbols to predict the formulas of ionic compounds formed by: (a) magnesium and oxygen, (b) aluminum and fluorine, (c) sodium and sulfur.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw Lewis symbols and determine electron transfer needs.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Mg: 2 dots (lose 2), O: 6 dots (gain 2); (b) Al: 3 dots (lose 3), F: 7 dots (gain 1); (c) Na: 1 dot (lose 1), S: 6 dots (gain 2)

step_by_step.explanation: Balance electrons lost and gained.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Mg loses 2, O gains 2 → 1:1 ratio; (b) Al loses 3, each F gains 1 → need 3 F; (c) Na loses 1, S gains 2 → need 2 Na

step_by_step.explanation: Write ionic compound formulas.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) MgO; (b) AlF₃; (c) Na₂S

step_by_step.explanation: Verify charge balance in each compound.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) MgO: Mg loses 2 e⁻, O gains 2 e⁻ (1:1 ratio). (b) AlF₃: Al loses 3 e⁻, 3 F atoms each gain 1 e⁻. (c) Na₂S: 2 Na atoms each lose 1 e⁻, S gains 2 e⁻. Lewis symbols predict ionic formulas by balancing electrons transferred.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using Lewis dot symbols to predict ionic compound formulas from electron transfer

sections.section_title: Limitations and Exceptions of Lewis Dot Diagrams
sections.introduction: While Lewis dot diagrams are powerful predictive tools, they have limitations. Understanding when and why Lewis symbols don't apply ensures accurate bonding predictions and prevents misconceptions.

key_concept.definition: Lewis dot diagrams work best for main-group elements; limitations include transition metals (d-electrons), expanded octets (Period 3+), incomplete octets (Be, B), and molecules with unpaired electrons (free radicals).
key_concept.context: These exceptions don't invalidate Lewis theory but require modifications or alternative models (like molecular orbital theory) for accurate descriptions.

classifications.value: Lewis symbols don't accurately represent transition metal bonding
classifications.behavior: Transition metals can use d-electrons for bonding; Lewis symbols only show s and p valence electrons; coordination compounds require different representation.

examples.explanation: Fe Lewis symbol shows only 2 valence e⁻ (4s²), but Fe can form Fe²⁺ (loses 4s²) or Fe³⁺ (loses 4s² + 1 3d). Lewis symbols don't show d-electron participation.

image.alt: Iron Lewis symbol showing limitation for transition metals
image.caption: Transition metal limitation: Lewis symbols don't show d-electron involvement in bonding.

examples.explanation: Cu Lewis symbol shows 1 valence e⁻ (4s¹), but Cu forms Cu⁺ and Cu²⁺. Lewis symbols can't predict multiple oxidation states for transition metals.

classifications.value: Some stable molecules have atoms with fewer than 8 valence electrons
classifications.behavior: Be and B don't have enough valence electrons to complete octet without forming charged species; accept electron pairs from other molecules.

examples.explanation: Be has 2 valence e⁻, forms 2 bonds with H. Be has only 4 e⁻ around it (incomplete octet). Stable molecule despite not following octet rule.

image.alt: BeH₂ Lewis structure showing incomplete octet on Be
image.caption: Incomplete octet exception: Be stable with only 4 valence electrons.

examples.explanation: B has 3 valence e⁻, forms 3 bonds with F. B has only 6 e⁻ (incomplete octet). Reacts with NH₃ to complete octet: BF₃ + NH₃ → F₃B-NH₃.

classifications.value: Period 3+ elements can have more than 8 valence electrons
classifications.behavior: Elements with n ≥ 3 can use empty d orbitals to accommodate more than 8 electrons; enables compounds like SF₆, PCl₅.

examples.explanation: S has 6 valence e⁻, forms 6 bonds with F. S has 12 e⁻ around it (expanded octet). Possible because S is Period 3 with available 3d orbitals.

image.alt: SF₆ Lewis structure showing expanded octet on S
image.caption: Expanded octet exception: S has 12 valence electrons using d orbitals.

examples.explanation: P has 5 valence e⁻, forms 5 bonds with Cl. P has 10 e⁻ (expanded octet). Not possible for N (Period 2, no d orbitals).

classifications.value: Molecules with unpaired electrons cannot satisfy octet for all atoms
classifications.behavior: Cannot pair all electrons; one atom has 7 electrons instead of 8; highly reactive due to unpaired electron seeking to pair.

examples.explanation: N has 5 e⁻, O has 6 e⁻, total = 11 e⁻ (odd). N has 7 e⁻ around it (incomplete). Paramagnetic (unpaired e⁻). Important signaling molecule in biology.

image.alt: NO Lewis structure showing unpaired electron
image.caption: Free radical exception: NO has 11 valence electrons, one unpaired.

examples.explanation: N has 5 e⁻, 2 O have 12 e⁻, total = 17 e⁻ (odd). N has 7 e⁻ around it. Brown gas, air pollutant, paramagnetic. Dimerizes to N₂O₄ to pair electrons.

principles.statement: Lewis dot diagrams are most accurate for main-group elements (Groups 1-2, 13-18) in Periods 2-3 forming simple ionic or covalent compounds.
principles.application: Use Lewis symbols confidently for NaCl, H₂O, CH₄, NH₃, CO₂; use with caution or alternative models for transition metals, expanded octets, and radicals.
principles.example: Excellent for: NaCl, MgO, H₂O, CH₄, NH₃. Limited for: FeCl₃, SF₆, NO, coordination compounds.
principles.analogy: Like a map: Lewis symbols are excellent for local navigation (simple molecules) but need different tools for complex terrain (transition metals, radicals).

principles.statement: Some molecules have multiple valid Lewis structures (resonance); actual structure is a hybrid, not switching between forms.
principles.application: Explains why O₃ (ozone) has equal O-O bond lengths despite Lewis structures showing single and double bonds; why benzene is unusually stable.
principles.example: Ozone (O₃): two resonance structures with different double bond positions; actual molecule has two identical bonds intermediate between single and double.

image.alt: Resonance structures for ozone showing delocalized bonding
image.caption: Resonance exception: actual structure is hybrid of multiple Lewis structures, not switching between them.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Lewis Symbol Limitations
worked_examples.problem: For each of the following, determine whether Lewis dot symbols accurately represent the bonding: (a) NaCl, (b) FeCl₃, (c) SF₆, (d) H₂O. Explain any limitations.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze NaCl (ionic compound, main-group elements).
step_by_step.calculation: Na (Group 1) + Cl (Group 17): both main-group, simple electron transfer. Lewis symbols work well ✓

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze FeCl₃ (transition metal compound).
step_by_step.calculation: Fe is transition metal: d-electrons participate, variable oxidation states. Lewis symbols limited ✗

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze SF₆ (expanded octet compound).
step_by_step.calculation: S is Period 3, forms 6 bonds (12 e⁻). Expanded octet possible but beyond simple Lewis prediction. Lewis symbols need modification △

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze H₂O (simple covalent molecule).
step_by_step.calculation: H and O are main-group, follow octet/duet rules. Lewis symbols work well ✓

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) NaCl: Lewis symbols work well (main-group ionic). (b) FeCl₃: Lewis symbols limited (transition metal, d-electrons). (c) SF₆: Lewis symbols need modification (expanded octet, 12 e⁻ on S). (d) H₂O: Lewis symbols work well (main-group covalent). Lewis dot diagrams are most reliable for main-group elements following octet rule.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Evaluating when Lewis dot symbols accurately represent bonding vs. when limitations apply

summary: Lewis dot diagrams represent valence electrons as dots around element symbols, providing a visual method to predict bonding behavior. Valence electrons equal group number (Groups 1-2) or group number minus 10 (Groups 13-18). Dots are placed singly on four sides before pairing; unpaired dots indicate bonding capacity. Metals (1-3 valence e⁻) lose electrons to form cations (no dots shown, charge in brackets); nonmetals (5-7 valence e⁻) gain electrons to form anions (8 dots shown, charge in brackets). Lewis symbols predict molecular formulas by matching unpaired electrons: C (4 unpaired) forms CH₄; N (3 unpaired) forms NH₃; O (2 unpaired) forms H₂O. Ionic formulas balance electrons transferred: Mg (loses 2) + O (gains 2) → MgO. Limitations include: transition metals (d-electrons not shown), incomplete octets (Be: 4 e⁻, B: 6 e⁻), expanded octets (Period 3+ can exceed 8 e⁻), and free radicals (odd electron count). Lewis symbols work best for main-group elements in simple ionic and covalent compounds. Resonance occurs when multiple valid Lewis structures exist; actual structure is a hybrid. Understanding Lewis dot diagrams enables prediction of ion charges, molecular formulas, bond types, and recognition of when alternative models are needed for accurate bonding description.
